intro: Most weekly reports are flat tables buried in email threads. Nobody reads them carefully, and the trends they contain go unnoticed. Turning your weekly report into a visual dashboard fixes that problem fast, and tools like Distill make the conversion almost instant by generating charts from a simple CSV upload.

whatIs.heading: What Is a Weekly Report Visual Dashboard?
whatIs.body: A weekly report visual dashboard is a collection of charts and summaries that represent the same data you would normally share in a spreadsheet or email table. Instead of rows and columns, your audience sees bar charts, line graphs, and key metrics at a glance. It works best with structured, recurring data like sales figures, support tickets, marketing performance, or project status updates.

whenToUse.heading: When Should You Use a Weekly Report Visual Dashboard?
bullets: When your team skims or ignores the spreadsheet you send every Monday morning.
When leadership wants a quick status overview without reading through rows of numbers.
When you need to spot trends across weeks, like declining response times or rising costs, and a flat table makes that difficult.

excelSteps.heading: How to Turn a Weekly Report into a Visual Dashboard in Excel
steps: Open your weekly report spreadsheet and make sure each column has a clear header (Date, Metric, Value, Category, etc.).
Select the data range you want to visualize. Include headers but exclude any summary rows at the bottom.
Go to Insert > Chart and pick a chart type. Line charts work well for trends over time. Bar charts work for comparing categories.
Repeat the process for each metric you want to display. Create one chart per key metric to keep things readable.
Move all charts to a new sheet. Right-click each chart, select Move Chart, and choose a dedicated 'Dashboard' sheet.
Arrange the charts on the Dashboard sheet so the most important metric sits at the top left. Resize them so everything fits on one screen.

sheetsSteps.heading: How to Turn a Weekly Report into a Visual Dashboard in Google Sheets
steps: Open your weekly report in Google Sheets and confirm your data has clean headers and consistent formatting.
Highlight the columns relevant to your first chart. For example, select Date and Revenue columns together.
Click Insert > Chart. Google Sheets will suggest a chart type. Switch to a line chart for time-based data or a bar chart for comparisons.
Use the Chart Editor sidebar to adjust titles, axis labels, and colors. Keep labels short and legible.
Create additional charts for each metric you report on. Place them all on a separate tab named 'Dashboard.'
Share the sheet with your team using the Share button. Set permissions to 'Viewer' so nobody accidentally edits the dashboard.

distillSection.heading: A Faster Way: Skip the Manual Steps with Distill
distillSection.body: If you already export your weekly report as a CSV, Distill can turn it into a visual dashboard in seconds. Upload the file and Distill auto-generates the right chart types based on your data structure. It also writes a plain-English narrative summarizing the key takeaways, so you get both the visuals and the story without building anything by hand. You can share the resulting dashboard with a link, which means no more attaching files or copying charts into slide decks.

gifAlt: Screen recording showing a weekly report CSV file being uploaded to Distill, which instantly generates a dashboard with line charts, bar charts, and an AI-written summary.

faqs.q: Can I do this without Excel?
faqs.a: Yes. Google Sheets handles the same workflow and is free to use. If you want to skip manual chart building entirely, Distill lets you upload a CSV and generates a full dashboard automatically. Both are solid alternatives depending on how much setup time you want to spend.

faqs.q: What chart types work best for a weekly report dashboard?
faqs.a: Line charts are the best fit for showing how a metric changes week over week. Bar charts are useful when you compare categories, like performance across team members or regions. If you track progress toward a goal, a simple gauge or progress bar can round out the dashboard.
Yes. Distill's free tier supports CSV files up to 1,000 rows and includes all chart types. No account is required to try it.
